title: FAANG Interview Patterns
description: Comprehensive Python data structures guide for FAANG interviews with diagrams, complexity tables, and interview patterns.

sections.title: Introduction & Learning Roadmap
sections.description:
What Are Data Structures?

Data structures are specialized formats for organizing, processing, retrieving, and storing data. Choosing the right data structure can mean the difference between an O(n²) brute force and an O(n log n) optimal solution — the very distinction that FAANG interviewers evaluate.

Why Python for DSA Interviews?

  • Expressive syntax — write solutions in fewer lines than Java/C++
  • Built-in batteries — dict, set, deque, heapq ready to use
  • No boilerplate — focus on algorithm logic, not memory management
  • Widely accepted — all FAANGs allow Python in interviews

Recommended Learning Sequence

Arrays & Strings
arrow_downward
Hash Maps & Sets
arrow_downward
Linked Lists
arrow_downward
Stacks & Queues
arrow_downward
Trees & BSTs
arrow_downward
Heaps
arrow_downward
Graphs
arrow_downward
Tries
arrow_downward
Union-Find
arrow_downward
Segment & Fenwick Trees
arrow_downward
Advanced Structures

# Start here: Install Python 3.8+
# These are the collections you'll use daily:
from collections import deque, Counter, defaultdict
import heapq

# Verify your setup
print("Ready for FAANG!")

sections.title: 1. Linear Data Structures
sections.description:
Elements are arranged sequentially — each element has a predecessor and successor. These form the foundation of most coding interviews.

Arrays (Python list)Frequently AskedO(1) access

Definition: Dynamic contiguous array — the most fundamental data structure. Python's list is a dynamic array that grows and shrinks automatically.

idx 1

idx 2

idx 3
40

Appendarr.append(v)O(1) amortized
Insertarr.insert(i, v)O(n)
Deletepop() / remove()O(n)
Searchv in arrO(n)

Linked ListsFrequently AskedO(n) search

Definition: A sequence of nodes where each node stores data and a pointer to the next node. No contiguous memory — insertions/deletions at head are O(1).

sections.codeBlock:
# === Linear Data Structures ===

# Array (list)
arr = [1, 2, 3]
arr.append(4)          # O(1)
x = arr.pop()          # O(1)

# Linked List Node
class ListNode:
    def __init__(self, val=0, next=None):
        self.val = val
        self.next = next

# Reverse Linked List
def reverse(head):
    prev, curr = None, head
    while curr:
        nxt = curr.next
        curr.next = prev
        prev, curr = curr, nxt
    return prev

# Stack / Queue / Deque
from collections import deque
stack = []; stack.append(1); stack.pop()
q = deque(); q.append(1); q.popleft()
d = deque(); d.append(1); d.appendleft(2)

sections.title: 2. Hash-Based Data Structures
sections.description:
Hash-based structures provide O(1) average lookups — the backbone of most FAANG solutions requiring fast membership tests, frequency counting, or caching.

Dictionary (dict)Frequently AskedO(1) all ops

Definition: Hash table mapping keys to values. Python's dict is the most versatile interview tool — use it for counters, caches, graphs, and memoization.

Key
→
Hash Function
→
Bucket
"apple" → hash() → bucket[3] → 10
Operations
OperationAverageWorst
InsertO(1)O(n)
LookupO(1)
DeleteO(1)
FAANG Patterns
Frequency CountingGroupingCaching / MemoizationPrefix Sum Hashing
Practice
Two SumGroup AnagramsSubarray Sum K
SetFrequently AskedO(1) membership

Definition: Unordered collection of unique elements. Use for deduplication, intersection/union operations, and visited-node tracking in graphs.

Key Operations
OperationComplexity
Add / RemoveO(1)
MembershipO(1)
Union (a | b)O(n)
Intersection (a & b)O(min(n,m))
FAANG Patterns
Duplicate DetectionVisited Nodes (BFS/DFS)Graph Algorithms
Practice
Contains DuplicateLongest Consecutive
Bonus: collections helpers
Counter

Frequency counting with most_common()

defaultdict

Auto-default values, great for adjacency lists

OrderedDict

LRU Cache — move_to_end()
sections.codeBlock:
# === Hash-Based Structures ===

# Dictionary - O(1) all operations
d = {}
d['key'] = 'value'
val = d.get('key', None)

# Set operations
a, b = {1, 2, 3}, {3, 4, 5}
print(a | b)  # Union
print(a & b)  # Intersection
print(3 in a) # Membership O(1)

# Collections helpers
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

# Count characters
counts = Counter('mississippi')
print(counts.most_common(2))  # [('i', 4), ('s', 4)]

# Auto-default list
adj = defaultdict(list)
adj[0].append(1)  # No KeyError

sections.title: 3. Tree Data Structures
sections.description:
Hierarchical structures with parent-child relationships. Trees are ubiquitous in FAANG interviews — binary trees, BSTs, heaps, and tries appear in nearly every onsite.

Binary TreeFrequently AskedO(n) traversal

Definition: Each node has at most two children (left and right). Master recursive traversals — they form the basis for 80% of tree problems.

Trie (Prefix Tree)AdvancedO(m) operations

Definition: Tree where each node represents a character. Enables O(m) prefix search (m = word length). Excellent for autocomplete and word games.

sections.title: 4. Graph Data Structures
sections.description:
Graphs model relationships between entities — the most versatile FAANG topic. Master adjacency lists, DFS/BFS, and the core algorithms below.

Graph (Adjacency List)Frequently AskedO(V+E) traversal

Definition: Vertices connected by edges. Python uses adjacency lists (defaultdict(list)) for efficient traversal.

0
→ 1→ 2
1
→ 0
2
→ 0→ 3
3
→ 2
0 ── 1
│
2 ── 3
Algorithms to Master

Floyd-Warshall

Kruskal's (DSU)
Tarjan's (SCC)

sections.title: 5. Advanced Data Structures
sections.description:
These structures appear in harder FAANG problems (typically on-site rounds). Master them after the core topics above.

Disjoint Set UnionAdvancedO(α(n)) ops

Definition: Tracks connected components with near-constant time union/find operations using path compression and union by rank. Also called Union-Find.

Operations
OperationComplexity
FindO(α(n))
UnionO(α(n))
Connected?O(α(n))

α(n) is the inverse Ackermann function — effectively constant for all practical inputs.

Applications
Number of IslandsConnected ComponentsMST (Kruskal's)
Practice
Number of ProvincesAccounts Merge
Segment TreeAdvancedO(log n) query

Definition: Tree that stores interval/range information. Each node represents a segment. Supports O(log n) range queries (sum, min, max) and point updates.

[0-7]
[0-3][4-7]
Complexities
OperationComplexity
BuildO(n)
Range QueryO(log n)
Point UpdateO(log n)
Fenwick Tree (BIT)AdvancedO(log n) ops

Definition: Binary Indexed Tree — simpler than Segment Tree for prefix sum queries. Each index stores the sum of a range determined by the least significant set bit.

Complexities
OperationComplexity
Prefix SumO(log n)
Point UpdateO(log n)
Practice
Range Sum Query
sections.codeBlock:
# === Advanced Data Structures ===

# Disjoint Set Union (Union-Find)
class DSU:
    def __init__(self, n):
        self.parent = list(range(n))
        self.rank = [0] * n

    def find(self, x):
        while self.parent[x] != x:
            self.parent[x] = self.parent[self.parent[x]]  # Path compression
            x = self.parent[x]
        return x

    def union(self, x, y):
        rx, ry = self.find(x), self.find(y)
        if rx == ry: return False
        # Union by rank
        if self.rank[rx] < self.rank[ry]:
            self.parent[rx] = ry
        elif self.rank[rx] > self.rank[ry]:
            self.parent[ry] = rx
        else:
            self.parent[ry] = rx
            self.rank[rx] += 1
        return True

dsu = DSU(5)
dsu.union(0, 1)
dsu.union(2, 3)
print(dsu.find(0) == dsu.find(1))  # True
